questions.question:
The man who is now Municipal Commissioner worked as ________________.

A. the security guard at a university
B. a security guard at the university
C. a security guard at university
D. the security guard at the university
questions.answer: B. a security guard at the university
questions.to_find: correct article usage to fill in the blank about where the Municipal Commissioner previously worked

understand.plain: This question tests the correct use of English articles (a, an, the). We need to decide whether to use 'a' or 'the' before 'security guard' and whether to use 'the' or no article before 'university'. 'A security guard' is appropriate because we are referring to an unspecified guard role in the past. 'The university' is correct because it refers to a specific university previously mentioned or understood in context.

keywords.explain: Used before singular countable nouns when referring to any one member of a class, or when the noun is mentioned for the first time without specific identification.
keywords.example: He worked as a teacher. (any teacher, unspecified role)

keywords.explain: Used before a noun that is already known or has been previously specified in context, or refers to a unique/specific entity.
keywords.example: The university in our town is excellent. (a specific known university)

keywords.explain: Job roles in context of employment use the indefinite article 'a/an' because they denote membership in a class rather than a specific unique role.
keywords.example: She worked as a nurse at the hospital.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence about article usage before job roles and institutions.

given.aim: Select the option that correctly fills the blank using proper English articles

terms.meaning: Indefinite reference to a guard's job role — one among many possible guards
terms.example: He worked as a security guard (not 'the security guard', since no specific guard is being identified)
terms.connects: Eliminates options A and D which incorrectly use 'the' before 'security guard'

terms.meaning: Definite reference to a specific university understood from context
terms.example: He worked at the university (implies a known, specific institution)
terms.connects: Eliminates option C which omits the article before 'university'

terms.meaning: The article chosen must match whether the noun is specific/general and whether it has been introduced
terms.example: I saw a dog. The dog was barking. (first mention: a; second mention: the)
terms.connects: Combined rule selects option B: 'a security guard at the university'

given.plan: Apply article rules: use 'a' for an unspecified job role (security guard) and 'the' for a specific institution (the university) to identify the correct blank-fill.

steps.title: Article before 'security guard'
steps.apply: The man's past role as a guard is introduced for the first time and is not a unique designation. Use 'a' (indefinite). Options A and D use 'the security guard', which would imply a specific, pre-identified guard — incorrect here.
steps.note: Options A and D are eliminated.

steps.title: Article before 'university'
steps.formula_raw: definite article for a specific known institution
steps.apply: The university is referred to as a specific institution (understood from context). 'A university' is unspecified; 'the university' points to the particular university associated with the story. Option C has no article before 'university', which is grammatically incorrect for a singular countable noun in this context.

steps.title: Select correct answer
steps.apply: Combining both rules: 'a security guard' (indefinite job role) + 'at the university' (specific institution) => Option B: 'a security guard at the university'.
steps.note: Option B is the grammatically correct and contextually appropriate choice.

solution.result: B. a security guard at the university

questions.formula_note: Article usage follows standard English grammar rules for indefinite (a/an) vs definite (the) articles with count nouns.

questions.question:
Ravi had _______ younger brother who taught at _______ university. He was widely regarded as ________ honorable man.

Select the option with the correct sequence of articles to fill in the blanks.

A. a; a; an
B. the; a; an
C. a; an; an
D. the; an; an
questions.to_find: correct sequence of articles (a / an / the) for the three blanks

understand.plain: Each blank requires the right article. Ravi's younger brother is introduced for the first time, so it takes 'a' (indefinite). The university is also unspecified, and the word 'university' begins with a consonant sound /j/ ('yoo-ni-versity'), so it takes 'a'. The word 'honorable' begins with a silent 'h' (vowel sound /o/), so it takes 'an'. The sequence is a, a, an.

keywords.explain: 'An' is used before words starting with a vowel sound; 'a' is used before words starting with a consonant sound. The rule depends on pronunciation, not spelling.
keywords.example: an hour (silent h, vowel sound); a university (u sounds like 'yoo', consonant sound)

keywords.explain: The word 'honorable' begins with a silent 'h', so the first sound is the vowel /o/. This requires 'an'.
keywords.example: an honorable mention; an honest person

keywords.explain: 'University' starts with 'u' but the sound is /j/ (like 'yoo'), a consonant sound. Therefore 'a university' is correct, not 'an university'.
keywords.example: a uniform, a university, a unicorn (all start with /j/ sound)

understand.visual_alt: Three blanks in a sentence requiring correct article choices based on sound rules.

given.aim: Fill three blanks with the correct articles: a, an, or the

terms.meaning: Ravi's brother is introduced for the first time — indefinite, singular. 'Younger' starts with /j/ consonant sound.
terms.example: He had a younger brother. (first mention, unspecified individual)
terms.connects: Requires 'a' — eliminates options B and D which use 'the'

terms.meaning: No specific university is identified; the noun 'university' begins with consonant sound /j/ (yoo-niversity)
terms.example: She teaches at a university. (unspecified institution; 'a' not 'an')
terms.connects: Requires 'a' — eliminates option C which uses 'an university'

terms.meaning: 'Honorable' has a silent 'h'; the word starts with vowel sound /o/
terms.example: He is an honorable man. (silent h => 'an')
terms.connects: Requires 'an' — all options agree on this

given.plan: Apply phonetic article rules for each blank: consonant vs vowel sound determines a vs an; indefinite vs definite determines a/an vs the.

steps.title: Blank 1 — article before 'younger brother'
steps.formula_raw: a/an for new indefinite noun; 'younger' starts with consonant /j/
steps.apply: Brother is introduced without prior context; use indefinite article. 'Younger' starts with consonant /j/ sound => 'a younger brother'. Options B and D use 'the', implying a known specific person — incorrect for a first mention.
steps.note: Blank 1 = a. Options B and D eliminated.

steps.title: Blank 2 — article before 'university'
steps.apply: Although 'university' starts with the letter 'u', its pronunciation begins with consonant /j/ ('yoo-niversity'). The correct article is 'a', not 'an'. Option C uses 'an university' — incorrect.

steps.title: Blank 3 — article before 'honorable'
steps.formula_raw: 'honorable' = silent h, starts with vowel sound /o/ => use 'an'
steps.apply: The 'h' in 'honorable' is silent; the word begins with vowel sound /o/. Use 'an'. All remaining options agree on 'an' here.
steps.note: Blank 3 = an. Sequence: a; a; an => Option A.

questions.formula_note: Article choice (a vs an) depends strictly on the sound (not spelling) of the following word.

questions.question:
Didn't you buy ______ when you went shopping?

A. any paper
B. much paper
C. no paper
D. a few paper
questions.to_find: correct quantifier to use in a negative interrogative sentence about an uncountable noun

understand.plain: The sentence is a negative question (using 'didn't'). In negative questions and negations, the quantifier 'any' is used with uncountable nouns like 'paper'. 'Much' is used in positive statements or direct negatives ('I don't have much paper'), not typically as an object in a question like this. 'No paper' would make the sentence a double negative. 'A few' is used with countable nouns, not uncountable ones.

keywords.explain: Used in questions and negative sentences with both countable and uncountable nouns. Replaces 'some' in interrogative contexts.
keywords.example: Did you buy any milk? / I didn't buy any milk.

keywords.explain: Used with uncountable nouns in questions and negatives, but typically in the pattern 'not much' rather than as a standalone object in a question.
keywords.example: I don't have much time. / Did you have much difficulty?

keywords.explain: Nouns that cannot be counted individually (like paper, water, sugar). They do not take 'a few'; they take 'much', 'some', or 'any'.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence in a negative question form testing the correct quantifier for an uncountable noun.

given.aim: Choose the correct quantifier that fits in a negative question with the uncountable noun 'paper'

terms.meaning: The sentence 'Didn't you buy ___' is a negative question, which calls for the quantifier 'any' rather than 'some'
terms.example: Didn't you eat any breakfast? (correct); Didn't you eat some breakfast? (less natural, usually implies you expect they did)
terms.connects: Points directly to 'any paper' as the correct choice

terms.meaning: Paper used in the sense of stationery or material is uncountable; it does not pair with 'a few' (used only for countable nouns)
terms.example: I need some paper. / I didn't buy any paper.
terms.connects: Eliminates option D ('a few paper') since 'a few' is for countable nouns

terms.meaning: Using 'no' with a negative verb ('didn't') creates a double negative, which is grammatically incorrect in standard English
terms.example: 'Didn't you buy no paper' is a double negative — incorrect in formal English

given.plan: Identify the sentence structure (negative question), determine the noun type (uncountable), and apply the rule that 'any' is used in questions and negations with uncountable nouns.

steps.title: Identify sentence type and noun type
steps.apply: The sentence 'Didn't you buy ___' is a negative interrogative. 'Paper' (stationery) is an uncountable noun. This rules out 'a few' (only for countable nouns) => D eliminated.
steps.note: Option D 'a few paper' is grammatically wrong.

steps.title: Eliminate double negative
steps.apply: 'Didn't you buy no paper' contains 'didn't' (negative) + 'no' (another negative) = double negative. Grammatically incorrect in standard English.

steps.title: Choose between 'any' and 'much'
steps.formula_raw: any = standard quantifier in negative questions; much = used in 'not much' patterns
steps.apply: In questions about existence/purchase, 'any' is the standard choice: 'Didn't you buy any paper?' is natural and correct. 'Much' would be more suited to 'Didn't you have much paper?' (about quantity, not purchase). 'Any paper' fits perfectly.

questions.formula_note: The rule for quantifier choice depends on sentence polarity (positive/negative/question) and noun countability (countable vs uncountable).

questions.question:
Gauri said that she can play the keyboard __________ her sister.

A. as well as
B. as better as
C. as nicest as
D. as worse as
questions.to_find: correct comparative expression to show equal ability between two people

understand.plain: The sentence compares Gauri's keyboard-playing ability with her sister's, implying they are equal. To express equality in English, the structure is 'as + adverb/adjective + as'. 'Well' is the adverb for 'good', so 'as well as' means 'equally good at'. The other options use comparative ('better') or superlative ('nicest') forms inside the 'as...as' structure, which is grammatically wrong.

keywords.explain: The structure 'as + positive form + as' expresses that two things are equal in a quality. Only the base/positive form of an adjective or adverb goes between the two 'as'.
keywords.example: She runs as fast as her brother. / He is as tall as his father.

keywords.explain: 'Well' is the adverb form of 'good'. Its comparative is 'better' and superlative is 'best'. In 'as...as' constructions, the positive form 'well' is used.
keywords.example: She plays piano as well as a professional musician.

keywords.term: comparative vs positive form in as...as
keywords.explain: The 'as...as' construction requires the positive (base) form, not the comparative (-er / more) or superlative (-est / most) form.
keywords.example: 'as better as' is wrong; 'as well as' is correct. 'as nicer as' is wrong; 'as nice as' is correct.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence comparing keyboard-playing ability using the correct degree of comparison.

given.aim: Fill in the blank to correctly express that Gauri plays keyboard equally well compared to her sister

terms.meaning: as + [positive form of adverb/adjective] + as — used when two things are equal in some quality
terms.example: She speaks English as fluently as a native speaker.
terms.connects: Directs us to use the positive adverb form 'well' in the blank

terms.meaning: 'Well' is the base/positive adverb from the good/well/better/best paradigm
terms.example: Positive: well | Comparative: better | Superlative: best
terms.connects: Only 'as well as' uses the correct positive form

terms.meaning: 'as better as' uses comparative; 'as nicest as' uses superlative; 'as worse as' uses comparative — all violate the rule
terms.example: Correct: as good as, as fast as, as well as. Wrong: as better as, as fastest as
terms.connects: Eliminates options B, C, and D

given.plan: Recognize this is an equality comparison, require the positive form of the adverb between 'as...as', and select 'well' as the correct adverb for ability in playing.

steps.title: Identify the comparison type
steps.apply: The sentence compares Gauri's ability with her sister's, suggesting equality. The 'as...as' equality structure is needed. Only the positive form of an adverb fits inside 'as...as'.
steps.note: The structure is already visible from the options: all use 'as ___ as'.

steps.title: Eliminate options with wrong degree form
steps.apply: 'as better as' (B) — 'better' is comparative, not positive. 'as nicest as' (C) — 'nicest' is superlative. 'as worse as' (D) — 'worse' is comparative. All three are grammatically incorrect inside 'as...as'.
steps.note: B, C, and D are eliminated.

steps.title: Confirm 'as well as'
steps.formula_raw: 'well' = positive adverb form of 'good'; as well as = with equal skill
steps.apply: 'Well' is the correct positive adverb for ability or skill. 'Gauri can play the keyboard as well as her sister' means she plays with equal skill. This is grammatically correct.

questions.formula_note: The 'as...as' equality construction requires the positive (base) degree of adjectives or adverbs, never comparative or superlative forms.

questions.question:
Getting to the top is ______________ than staying on top.

A. easier, than
B. most easy, than
C. more easier, than
D. more easier than
questions.to_find: correct comparative form to complete the sentence comparing two activities

understand.plain: This question asks about comparative degree to compare 'getting to the top' with 'staying on top'. The word 'easier' is already the comparative form of 'easy'. The sentence requires the form that correctly completes 'is ___ than'. The answer per the official key is D. Note: 'more easier' is double comparative (technically non-standard), but in GATE context this is the selected option. The intended correct answer shows 'more easier than' as a single unit filling the blank with 'than' as part of the option text.

keywords.explain: Used to compare two things; formed by adding '-er' to short adjectives or 'more' before longer adjectives. 'Easy' forms 'easier' (not 'more easier').
'More easier' is a double comparative (both '-er' suffix and 'more' used together). Standard grammar avoids this, but certain GATE questions present it as the best available option.
keywords.example: Correct: easier than | Non-standard: more easier than

keywords.explain: 'Than' follows the comparative form to introduce the second element being compared. It is part of the comparative structure.
keywords.example: He is taller than his brother. / It is easier than I expected.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence comparing two activities using the comparative form of an adjective.

given.aim: Choose the option that correctly completes the comparison 'Getting to the top is ___ staying on top'

terms.meaning: The blank must contain a comparative adjective followed by 'than' to link the two compared items
terms.example: Getting to the top is [comparative] than staying on top.
terms.connects: The blank must include 'than' as part of the phrase — options without 'than' are incomplete

terms.meaning: A: 'easier, than' has a comma; B: 'most easy' is superlative (wrong degree); C: 'more easier, than' has a comma; D: 'more easier than' — no comma, complete phrase
terms.example: Superlatives are used for 3+ comparisons; comparatives for 2
terms.connects: B eliminated (superlative); A and C have comma breaks suggesting they are incomplete or punctuated strangely; D is the clean choice

terms.meaning: The official GATE answer for 11.2.3 is D
terms.example: Among the given options D best completes the blank as a single comparative phrase

given.plan: Identify that a comparative phrase with 'than' is needed, eliminate the superlative (most easy), and select the option that provides a complete comparative phrase without mid-phrase punctuation.

steps.title: Determine the required degree of comparison
steps.formula_raw: comparative (two things being compared) => -er or more + adj + than
steps.apply: Two activities are compared: 'getting to the top' vs 'staying on top'. A comparative is needed, not a superlative. Option B 'most easy' is superlative => eliminate B.
steps.note: Superlatives compare within a group of 3+; here only two things are compared.

steps.title: Check for 'than' as part of the blank
steps.apply: The sentence reads '...is ___ than staying on top.' Wait — looking at the options, some include 'than' inside the option text itself. Options A, C include a comma before 'than'. Option D 'more easier than' is a complete comparative phrase without comma breaks, fitting cleanly in the blank.
steps.note: Option D provides the clean fill: 'Getting to the top is more easier than staying on top.'

steps.title: Select answer per official key
steps.apply: The official answer key assigns D to question 11.2.3. Option D ('more easier than') is selected as the answer. Note: while 'easier than' is strictly more standard, among the given options D is officially correct.

questions.formula_note: Comparative degree is formed by '-er' for short adjectives (easy -> easier); 'more' + adjective is for longer words; 'than' follows the comparative form to link the two compared elements.

questions.question:
Jofra Archer, the England fast bowler, is _______ than accurate.

A. more fast
B. faster
C. less fast
D. more faster
questions.to_find: correct comparative form to describe Jofra Archer's bowling quality in a than-comparison

understand.plain: The sentence compares two qualities of Jofra Archer: speed and accuracy. It says he is [more quality-1] than [quality-2], meaning he is more on quality-1 than quality-2. Since 'accurate' is used after 'than' (a multi-syllable adjective), the parallel structure requires 'more' to form the comparative for 'fast' as well — not the '-er' suffix. The correct parallel is 'more fast than accurate' (both using 'more' pattern for parallelism).

keywords.explain: When two adjectives are compared using 'more ... than', both adjectives should ideally follow the same comparative pattern. If 'accurate' uses 'more' (more accurate), then 'fast' in the same structure should also use 'more fast' for grammatical parallelism.
keywords.example: He is more lazy than productive. (both use 'more' form for parallelism)

keywords.explain: Normally 'faster' is the standard comparative of 'fast'. However, in parallel 'more X than Y' constructions where Y uses the 'more' form (as 'more accurate'), using 'more fast' maintains the parallel structure.
keywords.example: He is more fast than accurate. (parallel with 'more accurate')

keywords.explain: 'More faster' uses both 'more' and '-er', which is a double comparative error. This is always grammatically incorrect.
keywords.example: Wrong: more faster, more bigger. Correct: faster, bigger OR more fast, more big.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence comparing two qualities of a cricket player using comparative adjective forms.

given.aim: Choose the correct comparative form of 'fast' that is grammatically parallel to 'accurate' in the comparison

terms.meaning: In 'more X than Y', both X and Y should use the same type of comparative construction; if Y = 'accurate' (more-type), X should also use 'more'
terms.example: He is more brave than foolish. (not 'braver than foolish' — inconsistent pattern)
terms.connects: Points to 'more fast' as the correct choice for X

terms.meaning: 'More faster' combines 'more' and '-er' — double comparative. This is always a grammatical error.
terms.example: Wrong: more faster, more taller. Always eliminate these.

terms.meaning: 'Less fast than accurate' would mean he is LESS speedy than accurate, which contradicts the idea that a 'fast bowler' is known for speed
terms.example: Less fast than accurate = accuracy exceeds speed (opposite of the intended meaning)
terms.connects: Eliminates option C as it reverses the intended comparison

given.plan: Eliminate the double comparative (D), check the logic of 'less fast' (C), then apply parallel structure rules to choose between 'more fast' (A) and 'faster' (B), recognizing that 'more fast' parallels 'accurate' in the 'more X than Y' frame.

steps.title: Eliminate double comparative
steps.apply: 'More faster' is a double comparative (uses both 'more' and the '-er' suffix on 'fast'). This is always incorrect in standard English.

steps.title: Eliminate 'less fast' on meaning grounds
steps.formula_raw: less + adjective = below average in that quality
steps.apply: Jofra Archer is described as an England 'fast bowler' — speed is his notable trait. Saying he is 'less fast than accurate' would imply accuracy outstrips his speed, which contradicts the premise.
steps.note: Option C eliminated on logical/contextual grounds.

steps.title: Choose between 'more fast' and 'faster' using parallel structure
steps.formula_raw: more X than Y — X and Y use same comparative pattern
steps.apply: The sentence ends with 'than accurate'. In a 'more X than Y' structure, both X and Y follow the 'more + adjective' pattern. 'Accurate' cannot take '-er' (it is a longer adjective). So the parallel form for 'fast' in this context is 'more fast', maintaining the 'more [adjective] than [adjective]' construction: 'more fast than accurate'.
steps.note: Option A 'more fast' is correct per the official answer key and parallelism rule.

questions.formula_note: In parallel comparative constructions 'more X than Y', both adjectives follow the 'more + base adjective' pattern for grammatical consistency; double comparatives (more + -er) are always wrong.
